name: solid-principles
description: SOLID principles adapted for functional and TypeScript-first development.
capabilities: Explains Single Responsibility Principle with practical examples
Demonstrates Open/Closed Principle for extensible code
Shows Liskov Substitution for proper inheritance design
Teaches Interface Segregation with focused abstractions
Illustrates Dependency Inversion with dependency injection
author: DoubleslashSE
securitySummary: This is a pure documentation/educational skill containing only markdown explanations and TypeScript code examples demonstrating SOLID design principles. The static scanner misidentified TypeScript template literals as shell backticks, arithmetic operations as cryptographic algorithms, and code comments as system reconnaissance. No executable code, network calls, file system access, or environment variable access exists. Verified by reading SKILL.md (282 lines) and examples.md (387 lines) which contain only educational content.
valueStatement: SOLID principles help you write maintainable software. This skill teaches you to build testable, extensible TypeScript applications that adapt to changing requirements.
userTitle: Apply SOLID Principles to TypeScript Code
limitations: Educational content, does not automatically refactor code
Examples are in TypeScript, may not apply directly to other languages
Does not analyze existing codebases for violations

useCases.title: Learn SOLID fundamentals
useCases.description: Understand each principle through practical TypeScript examples and common violations

useCases.title: Review design decisions
useCases.description: Validate design choices and identify refactoring opportunities in legacy code

useCases.title: Study design patterns
useCases.description: Learn how SOLID principles guide object-oriented and functional design decisions

promptTemplates.title: Identify violations
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this TypeScript class and identify which SOLID principles it violates. Explain each violation and suggest a refactoring approach.

promptTemplates.title: Apply Single Responsibility
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor this class to follow the Single Responsibility Principle. Extract separate functions for each responsibility.

promptTemplates.title: Design extensible systems
promptTemplates.prompt: Redesign this switch statement to follow the Open/Closed Principle using strategy pattern or composition.

promptTemplates.title: Implement dependency inversion
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor this service to depend on abstractions instead of concrete implementations. Show the interface design.

bestPractices: Start with Single Responsibility - split functions by reason to change
Use dependency injection to invert dependencies and improve testability
Prefer composition over inheritance for flexibility
antiPatterns: God classes that handle multiple unrelated responsibilities
Deep inheritance hierarchies that break Liskov substitution
Fat interfaces that force clients to depend on unused methods

faq.question: Which SOLID principle should I learn first?
faq.answer: Start with Single Responsibility. It provides the foundation for clean code and makes other principles easier to apply.

faq.question: Can I apply SOLID principles to existing code?
faq.answer: Yes. Identify violations in critical classes and refactor incrementally. Focus on areas with frequent changes first.

faq.question: Does SOLID apply only to object-oriented code?
faq.answer: While designed for OOP, SOLID concepts translate to functional programming. Focus on maintainability and extensibility.

faq.question: How do I avoid over-engineering with SOLID?
faq.answer: Apply YAGNI. Use SOLID when you need actual flexibility, not for hypothetical future requirements.

faq.question: What is the relationship between SOLID and design patterns?
faq.answer: SOLID principles guide design decisions. Patterns like Strategy, Factory, and Repository help implement these principles.

faq.question: Can I use SOLID with TypeScript?
faq.answer: Absolutely. TypeScript interfaces and type system support SOLID principles well. This skill uses TypeScript examples.
